free_text: Comparison of Reactions of Copper-63 Compound Nuclei
Performed by Alpha-Particle, Proton, and Carbon-Ion
Bombardments
Carole M. Stearns, PhD thesis
submitted to Columbia University
Radiochemical techniques were
employed to separate, identify, and measure the Cu61,
Co57, Co58, and Co61 activities produced in the three
series of reaction studied. The radioactive species
were identified by comparing their observed half
lives with the literature values and their gamma-ray
spectra with standards.
Cumulative Errors.
BP
OS. REACTION and DECAY-DATA corrected in
subs.4,5,12, DECAY-DATA in sub.8
OS. REFERENCE updated
Brookhaven National Laboratory, 60-
inch cyclotron.
One irradiation was performed in the
internal beam of the Berkeley 60-inch cyclotron to
extend the alpha-particle excitation functions to an
energy of 48 MeV.
The targets were prepared by electrodeposition of
spectroscopically pure cobalt onto one-half mil gold
foils from a cobalt-ethylene-diamine solution. The
targets varied from 0.5 to 2 mg/cm2 in thickness, and
each target was uniform to +-2%.
Table I, pages 48-49.

The University of Minnesota linear
accelerator.
The 97.8%, enriched Ni62 was
supplied by Oak Ridge National Laboratory. The
thickness of the nickel deposit was determined as for
the cobalt targets and varied from 1 to 5 mg/cm2 with
a uniformity of approximately +-5% for individual
targets.
Table II, pages 50-51.

The Yale University heavy-ion linear
accelerator.
A stack of vanadium targets and interspersed aluminum
catcher foils. The vanadium deposits varied from 0.1
to 0.03 mg/cm2; each foil was of accurately known
area, and the thicknesses were determined by
quantitative analysis of the dissolved vanadium after
the irradiation. The uniformity is estimated to be
within -+ 6%.
Table III, pages 52.
